one_line_takeaway: OGPSA reduces the LLM alignment tax by projecting each safety-training gradient off a small low-rank 'general-capability' subspace, recovering utility (and modestly improving jailbreak robustness) more cheaply than replay across SFT/DPO/SFT-DPO on Qwen2.5 and Llama3.1.
key_finding: OGPSA improves the empirical safety-utility trade-off over naive tuning and over replay/merge/LoRA mitigations across both models and all three alignment stages. Under the hardest sequential SFT-DPO pipeline, Avg. Gain rises from 33.98% to 42.74% on Qwen2.5-7B and from 19.74% to 32.98% on Llama3.1-8B. Against advanced baselines (Qwen2.5-7B SFT) it reaches 39.06 Avg. Gain vs SFT 33.91, the CL method GPM 32.64, and the SOTA safety method STAIR 11.89. It is data-efficient (100-200 reference samples per dimension beat a 10k-sample replay) and cheaper than replay (2h56m / 4.39M input tokens vs +General Data 4h05m / 25.21M). It also lowers I-GCG jailbreak ASR relative to the corresponding baselines (SFT 32% -> 26%; DPO 54% -> 24%; base model 82%). Ablations show a rank-2 subspace (Helpful+Truthful directions) beats any single-dimension projection, and periodic subspace refresh beats a static projection.
core_contribution: The paper proposes OGPSA (Orthogonal Gradient Projection for Safety Alignment), a plug-and-play optimizer-level update rule that mitigates the 'alignment tax' (loss of general capability caused by safety post-training). Its own contribution is (1) reframing sequential safety post-training (SFT -> DPO -> RLHF-style stages) as an 'objective-heterogeneous' continual-learning problem in which safety gradients interfere with parameter directions supporting pre-trained general capabilities, and (2) a concrete update rule that, before each safety step, estimates a low-rank 'general-capability subspace' S_gen from gradients on small reference datasets and projects the safety gradient onto the orthogonal complement of S_gen, removing the component that would (to first order) increase the reference losses. The authors are explicit that their novelty is NOT the projection operator itself (they credit gradient-projection CL methods like GEM and GPM as prior/cited work) but its tailored formulation, subspace construction from broad general-capability reference gradients, and validation under objective heterogeneity across SFT/DPO/SFT-DPO. They provide a first-order 'steepest feasible descent' justification (Proposition 4.1) via Cauchy-Schwarz, framed as a local statement, not a global capability-preservation guarantee.
threat_model: This is a defensive AI-safety paper rather than an attack paper. The primary problem addressed is not an adversary but capability regression: safety alignment (harmful-query refusal, policy compliance) measurably degrades truthfulness, helpfulness, and instruction-following. Secondarily, the paper performs a defensive robustness check against jailbreaks: it verifies OGPSA does not open an optimization-based vulnerability by evaluating I-GCG (optimization-based jailbreak) on Qwen2.5-7B, and its StrongReject safety metric is scored under the PAIR and PAP-Misrepresentation jailbreak attacks. So the implicit adversary is a jailbreaker attempting to elicit harmful responses from the aligned model; OGPSA's claim is that it preserves (and modestly improves) refusal robustness while recovering utility, not that it defeats a specific new attack class.
limitations: Authors' stated limits: the method rests on a first-order LOCAL approximation, so it guarantees preservation only of the chosen differentiable reference losses, not of all downstream capability metrics; efficacy depends on reference-data diversity (a single reference direction is too narrow); and there is nonzero overhead (extra backward passes, minor cost in distributed settings). The paper is explicit that gradient interference is only ONE source of alignment tax (data curation, objective misspecification, refusal calibration, optimizer settings, benchmark sensitivity also contribute) and does not claim Pareto-optimality. Jailbreak robustness is stress-tested only with a single optimization attack (I-GCG); broader black-box, adaptive, and multi-turn evaluations are left as future work. Scalability is shown only within the Qwen2.5 family up to 7B. Reviewer-noted caveats: results are noisy per-benchmark (e.g., DPO+OGPSA drops WildChat and StrongReject on Llama3.1 relative to plain DPO), and the headline Avg. Gain aggregates heterogeneous benchmarks; the authors themselves warn against reading it alone.

datasets_used: PKU-SafeRLHF (10k samples, safety alignment data; refusal labels from gpt-4o-mini)
UltraFeedback (10k pairwise as replay baseline; 200 samples as Helpful reference dimension)
HaluEval (200 samples as Truthful reference dimension)
StrongReject (safety, scored under PAIR and PAP jailbreaks, goodness = 1 - rubric)
quantitative_results: Sequential SFT-DPO Avg. Gain: Qwen2.5-7B 33.98% -> 42.74% with OGPSA; Llama3.1-8B 19.74% -> 32.98%
Qwen2.5-7B SFT Avg. Gain: OGPSA 39.06 vs SFT 33.91, GPM 32.64, STAIR 11.89
Llama3.1-8B SFT Avg. Gain: OGPSA 31.50 vs SFT 20.19, +Merge 9.80, +LoRA 12.60, +General Data 7.24
I-GCG jailbreak ASR (Qwen2.5-7B): base 82%, SFT 32% -> OGPSA 26%, DPO 54% -> OGPSA 24% (lower is better)
Training cost (Qwen2.5-7B SFT): SFT 1h49m / 3.97M input tokens; +General Data 4h05m / 25.21M; +OGPSA 2h56m / 4.39M
Data-efficiency ablation (DPO): 100-200 reference samples per dimension recover IFEval/HHH better than 10k replay
Ablation: rank-2 (Helpful+Truthful) gives best balance (SimpleQA 3.35, IFEval 63.40, HHH 90.68) vs single-dim projections
Scalability (Qwen2.5-0.5B SFT): SimpleQA 0.09% -> 0.88%, Stereotype 79.31% -> 96.74% with OGPSA
baselines_compared: STAIR (SOTA safety-alignment framework using 20k mixed samples)
